title: Bohmian Trajectories in a Bistable Potential Well
abstract: We analyze the dynamics of a quantum particle in a one-dimensional bistable potential within the framework of Bohm's quantum mechanics. We give arguments that evidence the fallacy of certain claims found in the literature dealing with the impossibility of chaotic behavior of Bohmian trajectories in one-dimensional systems. We find that an appropriate choice for the initial position and wave packet causes the particle to undergo periodic, quasiperiodic, or chaotic motion. The transitions between these regimes occur in a continuos fashion.
summary: This paper explores the fundamental dynamics of Bohmian mechanics in a 1D system. It refutes the idea that 1D Bohmian trajectories are restricted from chaos by showing that a continuous bistable potential can produce periodic, quasiperiodic, and chaotic trajectories. By tuning the initial wave packet, the author demonstrates a continuous transition between these dynamical regimes. This provides new insights into the complex, non-linear nature of trajectories in the de Broglie-Bohm interpretation.
main_problem: Investigating whether Bohmian trajectories in a one-dimensional bistable potential can exhibit chaotic behavior, challenging existing literature that claims chaos is impossible in 1D systems.
main_result: The study demonstrates that Bohmian trajectories can undergo periodic, quasiperiodic, and chaotic motion, with transitions occurring continuously depending on the initial wave packet.
method: Numerical integration of the Bohmian guidance equation using a fourth-order Runge-Kutta algorithm, combined with Fourier analysis, stroboscopic (Poincare) plots, and Lyapunov exponent calculations.
model_or_system: A single quantum particle of mass M moving in a one-dimensional, analytically solvable, continuous bistable potential V(x).
key_observables: Particle position x(t), velocity, power spectrum P(f), phase-space portraits (Poincare plots), and the largest Lyapunov exponent.
important_parameters: Potential shape parameters (beta and xi), initial wave packet coefficients (cn), and the initial position of the particle.
main_assumptions: The dynamics are governed by the de Broglie-Bohm interpretation of quantum mechanics, specifically utilizing the effective potential V_eff = V + Q.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the classical, quantum, and effective potentials alongside phase-space plots. Fig 2 displays a trajectory, Poincare plot, and power spectrum for quasiperiodic motion. Fig 3 shows similar plots for chaotic motion. Fig 4 illustrates the time dependence of the largest Lyapunov exponent.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the bistable potential and Bohmian framework, presents numerical methods, details specific cases of periodic and quasiperiodic motion, demonstrates the emergence of chaos, and concludes with a Lyapunov exponent analysis to confirm chaotic regimes.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Potential as a function of the position, for ξ = 4. The dashed line is the bistable potential with maximum at the origin and minima at x = ±0.66. The system of units used is such that ℏ= M = 1, and the distances are measured in units of β−1. The dotted line is the quantum potential at t = 0, corresponding to the initial wave packet ψ(x) = iu0(x) + 10 u3(x). The solid line is the eﬀective potential Veff = V + Q. (b) Particle motion under the action of the eﬀective potential. The phase-space plots are for diﬀerent initial conditions: left (x0 = −0.5), center (x0 = 0), and right (x0 = 0.5).

FIG. 2. (a) Phase-space portrait of the motion of a quan- tum particle in a bistable potential with initial wave packet ψ(x, 0) = iu0(x)+u1(x)+10 u3(x) and initial position x0 = 0. The distances are measured in units of β−1 and ℏ= M = 1. (b) Stroboscopic view of the phase portrait. Positions and velocities are shown at times multiple of T = 2π/ω, with ω = (E3 −E1)/ℏ. (c) Power spectrum as a function of fre- quency f = ω/2π obtained from the time series of x(t).

FIG. 3. (a) Phase-space plot for a quantum particle in a bistable potential. The particle position at t = 0 is x0 = 0, and the initial wave packet is ψ(x, 0) = iu0(x) + u1(x) + 4 u2(x) + 10 u3(x). In the system of units used, ℏ= M = 1, and the lengths are measured in units of β−1. (b) Strobo- scopic view of the motion. Each point corresponds to the position and velocity measured at time intervals taken with a strobe frequency ω = (E3 −E1)/ℏ. (c) Power spectrum obtained from the time series of x(t) as a function of the fre- quency f = ω/2π.

FIG. 4. Upper curve: Largest Lyapunov exponent for a par- ticle in a bistable potential. The length unit is taken to be β−1, and in the system of units used ℏ= M = 1. The time dependence of the mean rate of exponential separation λ(t) was calculated from Eq. (11) (with t = n∆t) along a tra- jectory generated by the same initial conditions as those of Fig. 3(a). Lower curve: Largest Lyapunov exponent for the quasi-periodic motion shown in Fig. 2.
